rationale: The page contains detailed information about the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 targets, specific government schemes for education, and international/national university ranking frameworks. This is highly relevant for governance and social issues topics.
cleanedText:
Standards & Accreditation
- State-level Department of School Education for monitoring and policymaking.
Higher Education
- Quality Institutions: 3/4-year UG degrees with multiple exit options; Academic Bank of Credits (ABC).
- Teacher Education: Online training via SWAYAM/DIKSHA; National Mission for Mentoring.
- Regulatory Reform: Higher Education Commission of India (HECI) with four verticals.
- Other Areas: NCIVE for vocational integration; NETF for ed-tech integration.
Key Targets of NEP 2020
- Universal FLN (Foundational Literacy and Numeracy) in primary school by 2025.
- 100% GER (Gross Enrolment Ratio) in preschool to secondary level by 2030.
- 6% Public Investment in Education (% of GDP).
- 50% GER in higher education including vocational education by 2035.
- All higher education institutions (HEIs) to become multidisciplinary institutions, by 2040.
- At least 50% of learners through school and higher education to have exposure to vocational education by 2025.
Key Government Schemes/Initiatives under NEP 2020
- National Initiative for Proficiency in Reading with Understanding and Numeracy (NIPUN Bharat): Ensures FLN by end of Grade 3 by 2026-27.
- One Nation One Subscription: Central Sector Scheme for providing country-wide access to scholarly research articles and journal publication on a single platform.
- Initiatives for Children with Special Needs (CwSN): PM e-Vidya DTH Channel for Indian Sign Language; Disability Screening-PRASHAST (Pre Assessment Holistic Screening Tool) etc.
- PRERNA Program: Residential program for selected students of classes IX - XII focusing on experiential learning.
- Vidyanjali: School volunteer program encouraging community engagement and CSR participation.
- Rashtriya Vidya Samiksha Kendra: Provides real-time data on key performance indicators of school education for evidence-based decision-making.
- PM VidyaLakshmi Scheme, 2024: Enhance access to higher education through collateral-free loans for students enrolled in India’s top 860 institutions.
- SWAYAM Plus platform, 2024: Offers industry-aligned courses in emerging fields like AI and Data Analytics.
Related News: QS World University Rankings 2026
- Published: Annually by London-based Quacquarelli Symonds.
- Five Parameters: Research and Discovery, Employability and Outcomes, etc.
- New indicator named International Student Diversity has been added.
- Key findings of ranking:
- Five-fold increase: India grew from 11 universities in 2015 to 54 in QS World Rankings 2026, making it fourth most represented country after US, UK, and China.
- Leading new additions: 8 Indian universities added, more than any other country.
- IIT dominance: 12 IITs featured, with IIT Delhi ranking highest at 123rd globally.
Other Mechanisms for Higher Institutions Rankings in India
- National Institutional Ranking Framework (NIRF), 2015: Outlines methodology to rank institutions across country.
- Conducted by: Department of Higher Education, Ministry of Education.
- Parameters: Teaching, Learning and Resources, Research Practices, Perception, etc.
- All India Survey on Higher Education (AISHE): From 2010-11, it covers several parameters such as teachers, student enrolment, programmes, examination results, and education finance, infrastructure.
- Conducted by: Ministry of Education.
